content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to leveraging technology to enhance teaching and learning, prepare students for success in a digital world, and develop responsible digital citizens. Technology serves as a tool to support pedagogical goals, not as an end in itself. All technology integration must demonstrate clear educational benefit and align with the school's Schoolwide Learner Outcomes (SLOs), particularly Innovation and Communication.
content.title: 21.1.1 Vision Statement

content.text: The Digital Learning Strategy aims to achieve the following objectives over a 3-5 year horizon: Teaching and Learning Enhancement: Integrate technology meaningfully into curriculum delivery to personalize learning, increase engagement, support diverse learners (including EAL students), and develop 21st-century skills aligned with Cambridge International standards and Indonesian national curriculum requirements. Infrastructure Development: Maintain robust, reliable, and secure IT infrastructure that supports teaching and learning activities, administrative operations, and communication needs across all school contexts (day schools, boarding schools, urban and rural settings). Digital Literacy and Citizenship: Develop students' digital literacy skills and responsible technology use, preparing them to be ethical and effective digital citizens who understand online safety, privacy, and the responsible use of emerging technologies including artificial intelligence. Staff Professional Development: Build staff capacity to integrate technology effectively through ongoing professional development aligned with Cambridge's professional development pathways, including "Getting Started with AI in the Classroom" and "Teaching with Technology" modules. Data Security and Privacy: Protect student, staff, and institutional data through robust cybersecurity measures, compliance with Indonesian data protection regulations (UU PDP), and adherence to Cambridge secure materials handling requirements.
content.title: 21.1.2 Strategic Objectives

content.text: Digital learning initiatives are embedded within the School Improvement Plan (SIP) and monitored through EduNav dashboards. Technology-related KPIs are tracked as part of the school appraisal process under Domain 4: Educational Resources and Technology. Cross-Reference: See Section 4 for Strategic Planning and School Improvement framework.
content.title: 21.1.3 Alignment with School Improvement

subsections.title: EduNav Platform: School Information System and Learning Management System

content.text: EduNav is the integrated School Information System (SIS) and Learning Management System (LMS) deployed across all Eduversal Partner Schools. Developed and maintained by PT. Global Zerone Digital (Zerone), EduNav serves as the central digital platform for school operations, teaching delivery, and stakeholder communication. EduNav provides accounts for students, teachers, staff, and parents with customized access to relevant educational services. The platform supports student information management, learning evaluations, attendance tracking, grading, scheduling, and school administration.
content.title: 21.2.1 Overview of EduNav

content.text: Student Information System (SIS) Functions:
Learning Management System (LMS) Functions:

items: Resource library and digital content repository

content.text: EduNav implements role-based access control to ensure users can only access information appropriate to their role: Students: Access to personal academic records, course materials, assignments, grades, attendance records, and communication tools. Students can submit work, participate in discussions, and view feedback. Teachers: Access to class rosters, gradebook functionality, course management tools, communication features, and student academic information for assigned classes. Teachers are required to use EduNav-LMS exclusively for lesson delivery; use of alternative platforms is prohibited without explicit approval. Parents/Guardians: Access to their child's academic progress, grades, attendance, behavior records, and school communications. Parents receive automated notifications for significant assessments and communications. School Administrators: Full access to administrative functions, reporting tools, and system configuration. Access to student records is governed by need-to-know principles. Eduversal Central: Oversight access for quality assurance, appraisal data, and network-wide reporting while respecting data protection requirements.
content.title: 21.2.3 User Access and Roles

content.text: Teachers have specific responsibilities for effective use of the LMS:

items: Maintain current and organized course content aligned with curriculum standards
Provide clear directions and instructions when sharing materials
Update gradebook regularly with assessment results and feedback
Encourage students to use comment features for communication
Respond to student queries within reasonable timeframes
Participate in training programs for LMS usage
Report technical issues promptly to IT support
content.title: 21.2.4 Teacher Responsibilities in EduNav

content.text: Schools ensure data integrity through regular audits and quality checks:

items: Beginning of term: Enrollment and schedule verification
End of term: Final grade accuracy audit
content.title: 21.2.5 Data Quality Assurance

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 6.7 for Gradebook configuration and assessment reporting; Section 14 for Data Protection policies.
Schools maintain robust network infrastructure to support digital learning: Internet Connectivity:

items: Sufficient bandwidth for simultaneous use by students and staff
Redundant connections where feasible for continuity
Content filtering at network level (see Section 21.5)
Guest network for visitors separate from educational network
content.title: 21.3.1 Network Infrastructure

content.text: Wireless Network:

items: Campus-wide Wi-Fi coverage including classrooms, common areas, and boarding facilities
Credentials shared securely (posted in appropriate areas for authorized users)

content.text: Server Infrastructure:

items: On-premises servers for local applications (where applicable)
Cloud-based services for EduNav and backup systems
Regular maintenance schedules and update protocols

content.text: Standard classroom technology provision includes: Interactive Flat Panels (IFPs): Interactive Flat Panels serve as digital whiteboards enabling teachers to present material and engage students in interactive activities. IFPs feature:

items: Large touch-sensitive displays for visual instruction
Built-in educational software via Android Playstore
content.title: 21.3.2 Classroom Technology

content.text: Teachers receive training on effective IFP use to enhance visual learning and student engagement. Projectors and Display Equipment: Where IFPs are not available, schools provide projectors and screens for classroom presentation.
Eduversal Partner Schools utilize a managed device ecosystem: iPads: iPads serve as primary digital learning tools offering:

items: Wide range of educational apps and resources
Touch screen interface for intuitive interaction
Camera and multimedia capabilities for project work
content.title: 21.3.3 Device Ecosystem

content.text: Chromebooks: Chromebooks provide cost-effective options for:
Teacher Devices: Teachers are provided with devices (typically iPads) to:

items: Deliver content through IFP or projectors

content.text: All school-owned devices are enrolled in Mobile Device Management systems: Jamf (for Apple Devices): Jamf provides comprehensive MDM capabilities for iPads and other Apple devices:

items: Lost device tracking and remote wipe
content.title: 21.3.4 Mobile Device Management (MDM)

content.text: Apple Classroom App: Integrated with Jamf, the Classroom app enables teachers to:

items: Guide students to specific apps or websites
Lock student screens to maintain focus

content.text: Securly (for Chromebooks): Securly provides web filtering and classroom management for Chromebooks:
Student Responsibilities:

items: Handle devices with care and respect
Store devices in designated locations when not in use
Use only authorized applications and websites
Do not remove school property from campus without permission
content.title: 21.3.5 Device Care and Responsibilities

content.text: Teacher Responsibilities:

items: Always bring and use teacher device during lessons
Monitor student device use throughout instruction
Ensure devices are returned to charging stations

content.text: School Responsibilities:
Teachers must actively manage device usage:

items: Students should not use digital devices before teachers direct them to do so
Teachers use video lessons only in "live mode" to prevent unauthorized browsing
Teachers must always monitor student screens during lessons using Securly (Chromebook) or Jamf/Classroom (iPad)
Be cautious of students using earphones/headphones as they may be engaged in off-task activities
No tolerance for rule violations regarding device use in the classroom
content.title: 21.3.6 Device Usage During Lessons

content.text: Where schools permit personal devices: General Guidelines:

items: Personal devices may be permitted with parent consent and registration
Must comply with school network security requirements
Subject to same acceptable use policies as school devices
School not responsible for damage, loss, or theft
May be restricted to specific times or activities
content.title: 21.3.7 Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Policy

content.text: Registration Requirements:

items: Device details recorded with IT department
Security software installation may be required
Agreement to monitoring during school hours

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 22.8.3 for mobile phone confiscation policy.
The Acceptable Use Policy establishes guidelines for the appropriate use of school technology resources by all community members. The AUP applies to:

items: Personal devices used on school networks
Technology use during school-related activities (including trips)
Online behavior affecting the school community
content.title: 21.4.1 Purpose and Scope

content.text: Permitted Uses:

items: Educational activities assigned or approved by teachers
Collaboration with classmates on school work
content.title: 21.4.2 Student Acceptable Use Policy

content.text: Prohibited Uses:

items: Accessing inappropriate content (violent, sexual, illegal, or harmful material)
Sharing or viewing others' passwords or accounts
Attempting to bypass security or filtering systems
Using technology for cheating or academic dishonesty
Sharing personal information of self or others online
Accessing social media during instructional time (unless directed by teacher)
Gaming during school hours without teacher permission

content.text: AI Tools: Students may use artificial intelligence tools only within teacher-approved guidelines. Unauthorized or undisclosed AI use in assessments constitutes academic dishonesty. When AI use is permitted, students must:

items: Only use approved AI tools as specified by the teacher
Disclose AI assistance in their work
Maintain original thinking and personal voice
Not submit AI-generated content as their own work

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 6.4 and Section 22.8.2 for academic integrity policies regarding AI use.
Professional Use:

items: Technology use should primarily support teaching, learning, and administration
Personal use during working hours should be minimal and not interfere with duties
Staff must model appropriate technology use for students
All communications through school systems may be monitored
content.title: 21.4.3 Staff Acceptable Use Policy

content.text: Prohibited Uses:

items: Sharing student information through unauthorized channels
Using school resources for personal commercial purposes
Installing unauthorized software on school devices
Connecting unauthorized devices to school networks
Using personal social media to communicate with students

content.text: Social Media Professional Guidelines:

items: Maintain professional boundaries in all online interactions
Clearly distinguish personal opinions from school positions
Protect privacy and confidentiality of students and colleagues
Avoid forming personal relationships with students on social media
Communication with students should primarily occur through official school channels
Do not share sensitive or private school information

content.text: EduNav Access:

items: Use credentials securely and do not share
Access only information related to own child
content.title: 21.4.4 Parent/Guardian Access and Responsibilities

content.text: Communication:

items: Use official channels for school communication
Report concerns about technology safety promptly

content.text: Annual Agreement:

items: Students and parents sign AUP acknowledgment at enrollment and annually
Staff sign AUP acknowledgment upon employment and annually
content.title: 21.4.5 AUP Agreement Process

content.text: Communication:

items: AUP published in Student and Parent Handbook (bilingual)
Explained during orientation for new students and families
Regular reinforcement through assemblies and lessons

content.text: Violations of the AUP are addressed through the school's discipline framework: Minor Violations (Level 1): Verbal warning, educational conversation, temporary loss of technology privileges Moderate Violations (Level 2): Written warning, parent notification, suspension of technology privileges, detention Serious Violations (Level 3): Formal warning letter, extended loss of privileges, possible suspension Severe Violations (Level 4): May result in long-term suspension or expulsion, police referral if illegal activity involved Cross-Reference: See Section 22 for discipline procedures and consequences framework.
content.title: 21.4.6 Consequences for AUP Violations

content.text: Schools implement multi-layer content filtering to protect students from harmful content: Network-Level Filtering:

items: All internet traffic filtered through approved systems
Category-based blocking (violence, adult content, gambling, etc.)
URL blacklists for known harmful sites
content.title: 21.5.1 Content Filtering Strategy

content.text: Device-Level Filtering:

items: Apple Screen Time and restrictions on iPads

content.text: Activity Monitoring:

items: Web browsing history logged for managed devices
Alerts generated for concerning search terms or sites
Teacher visibility into student screens during lessons
content.title: 21.5.2 Monitoring and Reporting

content.text: Safety Alerts:

items: Automated alerts for high-risk content or behavior

content.text: Schools reserve the right to inspect devices for policy compliance: School Devices:

items: May be inspected at any time without notice
Students have no expectation of privacy on school devices
content.title: 21.5.3 Search and Device Inspection

content.text: Personal Devices (on School Network):

items: Subject to AUP when connected to school network
May be required to show screens when requested
Content inspection requires reasonable suspicion and administrator approval

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 22.8.4 for Search and Seizure policy.

Definition: Cyberbullying includes harassment, threats, humiliation, or intimidation through digital technologies including social media, messaging, email, online gaming, and other digital platforms. Prevention:
Response:

items: Report to homeroom teacher, counselor, or SC Coordinator
Immediate intervention to stop ongoing harassment

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 9.6 for cyberbullying within safeguarding framework; Section 22.8.1 for anti-bullying policy.
Digital citizenship is taught explicitly and integrated across subjects: Key Topics:

items: Healthy technology habits and screen time balance
content.title: 21.6.1 Curriculum Integration

content.text: Primary (Years 1-6):

items: Basic internet safety (strangers online, personal information)
Asking adults for help with technology problems
Understanding that not everything online is true
content.title: 21.6.2 Age-Appropriate Instruction

content.text: Lower Secondary (Years 7-8):
Upper Secondary (Years 9-12):

items: Advanced digital literacy and source evaluation
Social media impact on future opportunities

content.text: Schools provide parent education on digital citizenship:

items: Resources on parental controls and monitoring
Guidance on managing screen time at home
Updates on emerging technology trends and risks
content.title: 21.6.3 Parent Education

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 16 for parent engagement programs.
Schools implement comprehensive cybersecurity measures aligned with Indonesian cybersecurity regulations: Preventive Controls:

items: Data encryption in transit and at rest
content.title: 21.7.1 Security Framework

content.text: Detective Controls:
Corrective Controls:
Password Requirements:

items: Combination of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
Regular password changes (90 days for staff, annually for students)
No password sharing under any circumstances
content.title: 21.7.2 Password and Account Security

content.text: Account Management:
Backup Procedures:

items: Daily automated backups of critical systems
content.title: 21.7.3 Data Backup and Recovery

content.text: Data Retention:

items: Assessment data: Per retention schedule (see Section 14)

content.text: Incident Categories:

items: Category 1 (Critical): Data breach involving personal information, ransomware, complete system failure
Category 2 (High): Security compromise, significant service disruption, malware infection
Category 3 (Medium): Policy violations, minor security incidents, isolated failures
Category 4 (Low): Suspicious activity, minor technical issues
content.title: 21.7.4 Incident Response

content.text: Response Procedures:

steps: Detection and initial assessment
Containment and isolation
Notification to relevant parties (IT Coordinator, Principal, Eduversal Central for Category 1-2)
Investigation and evidence preservation
Eradication and recovery
Post-incident review and documentation
Lessons learned and policy updates

content.text: Data Breach Response: In case of data breach involving personal information:

items: Immediate notification to Principal and Eduversal Central
Notification to affected parties per regulatory requirements
Report to Indonesian data protection authorities if required

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 15 for Risk Management and Emergency Preparedness.

Eduversal recognizes artificial intelligence as an emerging tool that can significantly enhance teaching and learning when used ethically and responsibly. AI should complement, not replace, quality teaching and genuine student learning. The school's approach to AI balances embracing beneficial applications with maintaining academic integrity and safeguarding student development.
content.title: 21.8.1 Vision for AI in Education

content.text: For Teachers:

items: Lesson planning assistance and resource creation
Assessment design (not for grading without teacher review)
content.title: 21.8.2 Permitted Uses of AI

content.text: Teachers must always review and verify AI-generated content before use with students. For Students (When Explicitly Permitted by Teacher):
For Students:

items: Submitting AI-generated work as own work without disclosure
Using AI during assessments unless explicitly permitted
Bypassing learning processes through AI dependence
Using AI to complete homework or assignments intended for independent completion
Using AI to take tests or complete controlled assessments
content.title: 21.8.3 Prohibited Uses of AI

content.text: For Staff:

items: Writing student reports without substantial personal input
Grading student work entirely by AI without review
Making important decisions affecting students based solely on AI recommendations
Using AI that processes student personal data without proper authorization

content.text: When AI use is permitted:

items: Students must acknowledge AI assistance in their work
Description of how AI was used (research, drafting, editing, etc.)
All final work must reflect student's own understanding and voice
Original thinking and critical analysis must be demonstrated
content.title: 21.8.4 Disclosure Requirements

content.text: Failure to disclose AI assistance constitutes academic dishonesty.
Teachers receive professional development on AI integration aligned with Cambridge's "Getting Started with AI in the Classroom" workshop:

items: Practical tools and strategies for effective use
Assessment design in the AI era
content.title: 21.8.5 Teaching About AI

content.text: Students learn about AI through age-appropriate instruction:

items: Understanding what AI is and how it works

content.text: The relationship between AI and academic integrity is addressed through:

items: Clear policy communication to students and parents
content.title: 21.8.6 Academic Integrity and AI

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 6.4 for academic integrity; Section 22.8.2 for AI-related misconduct consequences.
Schools utilize vetted educational applications that support curriculum delivery:
New educational applications are evaluated against:

items: Educational value and alignment with curriculum objectives
Ease of use for students and teachers
content.title: 21.9.2 Application Selection Criteria

steps: Teacher or department submits request with educational justification
IT Coordinator conducts technical and security review
Data Protection Impact Assessment if personal data processed
Principal approval for school-wide adoption
Deployment through MDM for managed devices
Training provided for teachers
Annual review of continued use
content.title: 21.9.3 Application Approval Process

content.text: Teachers are required to use EduNav-LMS as the primary platform for lesson delivery. Alternative applications should complement, not replace, the LMS. This ensures:

This policy applies to all students, teachers, staff, parents, guardians, volunteers, and anyone representing the school in any official capacity on social media platforms, whether on-campus or off-campus.
content.title: 21.10.1 Scope

content.text: Respect and Civility: Students must maintain respectful and civil conduct when using social media. Cyberbullying, harassment, and hate speech will not be tolerated and will be addressed through discipline procedures. Privacy: Students must protect their own and others' privacy by not sharing personal information such as addresses, phone numbers, or private details on social media. Academic Integrity: Students must uphold academic integrity and avoid using social media for cheating, plagiarism, or any form of academic dishonesty. School Representation: Students should be mindful that their online behavior reflects on the school community, particularly when identifiable through uniforms, school names, or location tags.
content.title: 21.10.2 Student Use of Social Media

content.text: Professionalism: Teachers and staff must maintain high professionalism when using social media. Personal opinions expressed online should be clearly distinguished from school positions. Privacy and Confidentiality: Staff must protect the privacy and confidentiality of students, colleagues, and the school. No sensitive or private information should be shared. Boundaries: Staff must avoid forming personal relationships with students on social media platforms. Communication with students should primarily occur through official school channels (EduNav, school email). Images and Content: Staff must not post photos or videos of students without proper consent and must follow school media consent procedures.
content.title: 21.10.3 Staff Use of Social Media

content.text: Account Ownership: Official school social media accounts are owned and managed by the school. Only authorized staff members should have access to these accounts. Content Guidelines: All content shared on official school social media accounts should:

items: Align with the school's values, mission, and policies
Be approved by designated authority before posting
Present the school positively and professionally
content.title: 21.10.4 Official School Accounts

content.text: Monitoring: Official accounts should be monitored regularly for comments and interactions requiring response or moderation.
Schools integrate online and face-to-face learning to enhance educational outcomes: Blended Learning Models:

items: Flipped classroom: Content delivered online, class time for application and discussion
Station rotation: Students rotate through online and face-to-face activities
Enrichment: Online resources extend and deepen classroom learning
Remediation: Online tools support students needing additional practice
content.title: 21.11.1 Blended Learning Approach

content.text: Quality Indicators:

items: Technology enhances learning objectives, not technology for its own sake
Balance between screen-based and other learning activities

content.text: When circumstances require remote learning (emergencies, health situations, extended absence): Teacher Responsibilities:

steps: Follow established teaching schedules for remote lessons
Use approved technology tools (primarily EduNav, Google Meet/Zoom)
Maintain quality and engagement comparable to in-person instruction
Communicate regularly with students regarding schedules and expectations
Provide clear instructions and support for technical issues
Monitor student participation and follow up on non-engagement
content.title: 21.11.2 Remote/Online Learning Provisions

content.text: Student Responsibilities:

steps: Attend scheduled online sessions punctually
Participate actively and appropriately
Complete assignments within deadlines
Communicate technical difficulties promptly
Maintain appropriate behavior during online sessions
Create suitable learning environment at home

content.text: Parent Responsibilities:

steps: Ensure appropriate learning environment at home
Monitor student engagement where appropriate
Communicate concerns or difficulties to school
Support student in maintaining routine and focus

content.text: For synchronous online sessions:

items: Enable waiting rooms and require authentication
Teachers remain in control of sessions (mute, screen share permissions)
Recording only with appropriate consent and notification
Sessions scheduled during regular school hours where possible
Parent awareness of session schedules for younger students
content.title: 21.11.3 Video Conferencing Guidelines

content.text: Technology integration should follow these principles: Start with Learning Goals:

items: Consider how technology helps achieve specific learning objectives
Be intentional in choosing tools that align with instructional strategies
Plan technology use to support or enhance learning, not for technology's sake
content.title: 21.12.1 Principles of Effective Technology Integration

content.text: Introduce Technology Purposefully:

items: Provide clear instructions and expectations before introducing new tools
Model use of technology tools for students
Balance technology use with other learning activities

content.text: Engage Students:

items: Use technology to create engaging and interactive learning experiences
Encourage collaboration and communication through technology tools

content.text: Provide Accessible and Inclusive Learning:

items: Be aware of accessibility issues for students with disabilities or special needs
Use Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles
Address the digital divide and ensure equitable access

content.text: Choose Appropriate Tools:

items: Select tools appropriate for students' age and skill level
Younger students may require simpler tools

content.text: Technology integration is evaluated as part of school and teacher appraisal: LMS Usage (Domain 4.D):

items: Percentage of teachers actively using the LMS
Content organization and alignment with curriculum standards
Effective training for teachers, students, and parents
content.title: 21.12.2 Appraisal Criteria for Technology Integration

content.text: Technology Integration in Teaching (Domain 4.E):

items: Technology resources reachable in classrooms (computers, tablets, IFPs, internet)
Professional development programs on technology integration
Technology used to enhance learning objectives and diverse instructional strategies
Student engagement and interaction with technology
Innovative practices (flipped classroom, blended learning, project-based learning)
Technology use in formative and summative assessments

content.text: Digital Policy (Domain 4.F):

items: Availability and communication of digital policy
Policy monitoring and updates for emerging technologies

content.text: Teachers receive ongoing professional development in technology integration: Required Training:

items: EduNav/LMS usage (during induction and annual refresher)
content.title: 21.12.3 Professional Development

content.text: Recommended Training (Cambridge PD):

items: Getting Started with AI in the Classroom
Getting Started with Active Learning (technology components)

content.text: Ongoing Support:

items: IT help desk for technical issues
Access to training resources and tutorials

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 12.4 for DTP professional development framework.

Educational technology procurement follows these principles: Educational Value: Primary consideration is impact on teaching and learning Security and Privacy: Vendors must meet data protection requirements Total Cost of Ownership: Consider acquisition, implementation, training, and ongoing costs Sustainability: Ensure long-term viability and support Standardization: Preference for solutions that work across the network
content.title: 21.13.1 Procurement Principles

steps: Needs Assessment: Identify educational need and requirements
Market Research: Evaluate available options
Security Review: Data protection and cybersecurity assessment
Pilot Testing: Trial with limited group where appropriate
Financial Approval: Budget authorization per procurement policy
Contract Review: Legal review of terms and conditions
Implementation: Deployment and training
Evaluation: Ongoing assessment of effectiveness
content.title: 21.13.2 Procurement Process

content.text: Eduversal maintains partnerships with trusted vendors: PT. Global Zerone Digital (Zerone): EduNav platform development and support Bess Project (BP): Educational equipment supply including IFPs, furniture, and technology hardware Other Partners: Subject to evaluation and approval processes
content.title: 21.13.3 Vendor Partnerships

content.text: All vendors processing student or staff data must:

items: Demonstrate compliance with Indonesian data protection requirements
content.title: 21.13.4 Data Processing Agreements

items: Overall accountability for technology policy implementation
Approve technology-related expenditure within budget authority
Ensure staff and students comply with policies
Report technology matters to GM/Foundation Representative
content.title: 21.14.1 Principal

items: Day-to-day management of IT infrastructure and systems
User support and help desk management
content.title: 21.14.2 IT Coordinator/Manager

items: Promote effective technology integration in teaching
Support teachers in using digital tools
Evaluate educational applications for subject areas
Model good practice in technology use
Ensure curriculum includes digital literacy where appropriate
content.title: 21.14.3 Academic Leadership (HoDs, Subject Leaders)

items: Monitor and manage student device use during lessons
Report technical issues and security concerns
Maintain EduNav/LMS with current content and grades
Teach digital citizenship within subject areas
content.title: 21.14.4 Teachers

items: Report concerns about online safety or technology issues
Support peers in positive technology use
content.title: 21.14.5 Students

items: Support school technology policies at home
Monitor student technology use outside school
Support student in responsible device use
content.title: 21.14.6 Parents/Guardians

items: Provide EduNav platform and support through Zerone partnership
content.title: 21.14.7 Eduversal Central

content.text: This section is reviewed annually: Timeline:

items: September: Updated policy communicated and implemented
content.title: 21.15.1 Annual Review Process

content.text: Review Committee:
Data Analysis:

items: Technology usage data from EduNav and MDM systems
content.title: 21.15.2 Inputs to Review

content.text: Stakeholder Feedback:

items: Staff survey on technology support and effectiveness
Parent survey on communication and access

content.text: External Factors:
All updates documented with:
Previous versions retained for reference.

subsections.title: Key Evidence and Artifacts for Accreditation

content.text: Schools maintain the following documentation for CIS/WASC accreditation visits:

steps: Digital Learning Strategy document aligned with School Improvement Plan
Acceptable Use Policy (staff and student versions, bilingual) with signed acknowledgment records
Technology inventory and maintenance records
EduNav usage reports and system access logs
Cybersecurity audit reports and vulnerability assessments
Data backup and recovery test records
Incident response logs and post-incident reviews
AI usage guidelines and staff training records
Professional development records for technology training
Student and staff surveys on technology effectiveness
Content filtering reports and configuration documentation
Vendor contracts and data processing agreements
Device management policy and MDM configuration records
Digital citizenship curriculum and lesson materials

content.text: CIS/WASC Standards:

items: Domain D: Learning Environment and Technology Integration
Domain E: Educational Resources and Technology
Evidence of purposeful technology use to enhance learning

content.text: Indonesian Regulations:

items: UU No. 27/2022 concerning Personal Data Protection (UU PDP)
Government Regulation on Electronic Systems and Transactions (PP PSTE)
Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (Kominfo) cybersecurity guidelines

content.text: Cambridge Assessment:

items: Academic integrity requirements related to technology and AI
Professional development pathways for technology integration

content.text: Eduversal QMS:

items: Technology integration in EASE evaluation criteria
